intro:
The Epistle to the Colossians is one of Paul's prison letters, written during his Roman imprisonment (approximately AD 60–62) alongside Ephesians and Philemon. It addresses the church at Colossae, founded by Epaphras, and responds to a doctrinal deviation that diminished Christ's preeminence — possibly involving angel veneration, ascetic regulations, and mystical visionary practices drawn from a blending of Jewish and pagan elements. Paul counters this with the letter's theological centerpiece: the supremacy and sufficiency of Christ as the fullness of the Godhead in bodily form (Colossians 1:15–20; Colossians 2:9).

The letter divides naturally into a doctrinal section (chapters 1–2) and a practical section (chapters 3–4). The Christological hymn in Colossians 1:15–20 is among the most exalted descriptions of Christ in the New Testament canon. Paul instructs believers that having died and risen with Christ, their lives are hidden with Christ in God and should be ordered accordingly. The letter was to be exchanged with the letter to Laodicea after being read (Colossians 4:16), indicating that these early letters circulated among neighboring churches in the Lycus valley.
